title: Justice! Are You Still Alive?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's legal chronology, attributed allegations, quoted judicial observations, political directness, rhetorical questions, irony, figures, dates, and paragraph order. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which clear OCR character, spacing, and non-text scan errors are repaired. The canonical source remains unchanged and authoritative. The printed sign-off visibly reads `20-4-2017`; although the letter discusses the April 2014 parliamentary campaign and cites a meeting on 17 April 2014, the source-printed date is preserved rather than silently normalised. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: A petition filed in the Supreme Court seeking four months' time to try Jayalalithaa's income-tax case was taken up within a week. At the same time, some cases remain delayed for years. Why do such contradictions exist in the courts?

That was what I spoke about in detail at the election campaign meeting in Kanchipuram, citing Perarignar Anna's play *Needhi Devan Mayakkam* (*The God of Justice Bewildered*). For example, how many years has the disproportionate-assets case been proceeding before the Bengaluru Special Court? How many adjournments have been obtained? How many years has the case filed by the Income Tax Department remained under trial?

Four income-tax cases were filed against Sasi Enterprises. The firm was registered on 6 February 1989 with N. Sasikala and T.T.V. Dhinakaran as partners. On 4 May 1990, it was reconstituted with J. Jayalalithaa and N. Sasikala as its partners. Do you know what Sasi Enterprises was supposed to do?

It was registered to conduct business relating to every kind of car, as well as vehicles and goods. Poor thing! Think how hard our Chief Minister Jayalalithaa must have struggled, running enterprises such as a car dealership and earning revenue for that company! Yet this case arose because the firm wilfully and deliberately failed to file returns on the income it had collected.

Two cases concerned income-tax returns for the assessment years 1991-92 and 1992-93. Two more concerned returns for 1993-94. With the approval of the Chennai Income Tax Commissioner, the complaints underlying these cases were filed before the Additional Chief Metropolitan Magistrate in Chennai on 21 August 1997. Jayalalithaa and Sasikala filed petitions seeking discharge from the cases and then delayed without pursuing them, following which the Income Tax Department approached the High Court and the Supreme Court. On 4 September 2006, the Supreme Court directed the trial court to decide within four weeks their petitions seeking discharge. The Chief Metropolitan Magistrate accordingly heard and dismissed Jayalalithaa's petition. In other words, in a case filed in 1997, the accused sought discharge nine years later. What happened during those nine years? Nothing. That is all!
After the Egmore Magistrate dismissed their discharge petitions, Jayalalithaa's side appealed to the Madras High Court. That appeal too was dismissed by the High Court on 2 December 2006.

Jayalalithaa's side appealed against the High Court order to the Supreme Court. Hearing the appeal, the Supreme Court said that there was no basis whatever to interfere with the Madras High Court's order and no merit sufficient to entertain the appeal. On 30 January 2014, it directed the trial court to hear and complete the case within four months.

The High Court dismissed the matter on 2 December 2006. Jayalalithaa appealed to the Supreme Court, and that petition was dismissed eight years later. The case itself was filed in 1997. The Magistrate's Court dismissed the discharge petition in 2006, nine years later. The Supreme Court dismissed in January 2014 the appeal filed in 2006, after another eight years, and directed that the trial be completed in just four months.

In that judgment, the Supreme Court observed that the central issue was whether the burden of proof rested upon the Income Tax Department, which brought the charge, or upon the accused, Jayalalithaa and Sasikala. Since intention could be presumed in this case, it was for the accused to prove beyond reasonable doubt that they were innocent. They therefore had to establish before the trial court the circumstances in which they failed to file their income-tax returns:

“The question is on whom the burden lies, either on the prosecution or the assessee, under Section 278E to prove whether the assessee has or has not committed wilful default in filing the returns. Court in a prosecution of offence, like Section 276CC, has to presume the existence of mens rea and it is for the accused to prove the contrary and that too beyond reasonable doubt. Resultantly, the appellants have to prove the circumstances which prevented them from filing the returns as per Section 139(1) of in response to notices under Sections 142 and 148 of the Act.”
From this, one must remember that the burden of proving their innocence in the case rested upon Jayalalithaa and Sasikala.

It was in this very case, after writing all this and ordering completion within four months, that the same Supreme Court has now postponed the trial for another three months because Jayalalithaa must campaign in the parliamentary election. It also ordered Jayalalithaa and Sasikala to appear without fail on the date fixed by the trial court. Only four days of parliamentary election campaigning remain, yet the Supreme Court has granted three months. Who can question it?

Here is another incident. The case concerning Jayalalithaa and her friend Sasikala's failure to file income-tax returns for 1993-94 came before Supreme Court Justices B.N. Agrawal and A.K. Mathur on 24 February 2006. Jayalalithaa's lawyer sought six weeks' time.
Do you know what the judges said then?

“You are making a mockery of the administration of justice. How much longer can you drag out these court proceedings? You must appear before the court, answer questions under Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, and set an example to others. What counter-affidavit can you file in this matter? We wish to issue an order today itself directing your client to appear before the court and answer the questions put to her.”
*The Hindu*, dated 25 February 2006, reported under the heading “Court Pulls Up Jayalalithaa”:

“You [Jayalalithaa] are making a mockery of the judicial process. How long can you drag the proceedings? You must set an example to others by presenting yourself in court for questioning under Section 313 CrPC. What counter-affidavit can you file in this matter? We want to pass an order today itself directing your client to be present in court.”

Here is yet another case. In 1995, irregularities were alleged in the purchase of a device called a transponder for J.J. TV. Enforcement Directorate officials filed five cases, including one against Super Duper, a firm connected with J.J. TV. Sasikala and Bhaskaran are accused in this case. Examination of prosecution witnesses ended many years ago. Under Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, the Magistrate must then put to the accused questions prepared from the evidence and record their answers. The Magistrate adjourned the case to 28 April and ordered Sasikala and Bhaskaran to appear that day. The income-tax case against Chief Minister Jayalalithaa was also adjourned to that same date. Let us wait and see whether they appear at least then!

With all these events in mind, at the election meeting held in Kanchipuram on 17 April 2014 I raised this crucial question: “Has it come to this: the case may proceed only when we come; if we do not come, it must be adjourned; otherwise it must be withdrawn? Is this Jayalalithaa's realm, or the realm of justice?”
Pause for a moment and consider the kind of person who has become our Chief Minister!
She received a sentence of one year's rigorous imprisonment from the Special Court in 2000 in the Kodaikanal Pleasant Stay Hotel case!
She supplied information contrary to the truth, claiming that her vineyard in Andhra Pradesh earned Rs 60 lakh between 1987 and 1993 without any grapes being cultivated!
Although Jayalalithaa appealed in some of these cases and escaped the sentences at the appellate stage, I have listed here only the instances in which the trial court convicted her.
She failed to file income-tax returns for 1991-92 and 1992-93 and has obtained adjournment after adjournment for years!
She signed a file and later told the court that the signature was not hers!

In the 2001 Assembly election, knowing that she must not file nomination papers in four constituencies, she nevertheless did so to gain public sympathy! Knowing that her conviction made her ineligible to contest, she still contested! Defying the rule that a convicted person could not assume office as Chief Minister, she exercised power in that office for several months, until a Supreme Court judgment removed her!
While serving as Chief Minister, she celebrated her birthday and deposited gifts totalling Rs 2.15 crore into her personal account!

In the case concerning her purchase at a low price of land belonging to the government-owned TANSI, the Special Court sentenced her to three years' rigorous imprisonment and imposed a fine of Rs 10,000!
In the case concerning the purchase of another TANSI property for Sasi Enterprises, she was sentenced to two years' rigorous imprisonment and fined Rs 5,000!

In its judgment of 25 November 2003 in the TANSI case, the Supreme Court said: “Criminal law emphasises that persons in public life must maintain minimum standards of conduct and morality. Persons holding high office must, in such circumstances, act with the highest integrity rather than shelter behind the law. Those in public life must respect standards of conduct and not escape by hiding among the twists and turns of criminal cases. There is a growing tendency among persons in high office to increase their wealth using government property, or to convert what belongs to the Government into their own. We strongly condemn that tendency.” Our Chief Minister is the person who received this certificate from the Supreme Court!

Why, she claimed that she governed on a salary of just one rupee a month, yet within five years accumulated assets now worth Rs 5,000 crore, over which a case has been proceeding for years in the Bengaluru court.
That person of distinction, who keeps postponing the case through adjournment after adjournment, is our Chief Minister!
She has fielded AIADMK candidates in all forty constituencies, denied even one seat to any allied party, and is flying by “helicopter” to ask for votes without hardship.

I, at the age of ninety, travel hundreds of kilometres by road to meet the people in a van unsuited to travel, being thrown up and down along the way. Whenever the van falls into a pothole, the surgery on my back leaves me suffering in pain. The van struggles to move through the flood of people. At every meeting, the people's enthusiasm makes me speak for an hour. When I reach my lodging, my entire body aches. There is no sleep all night. Ask the two doctors travelling with me and they will tell you how much I suffer. If someone says I should rest for a day, or at least cancel one meeting, a hundred people immediately come to object.

I shall work as much as I can; I shall work! I know that you will make the right decision. Yet I trust that you will approach Tamil Nadu's voters and labour to make them vote for the candidates of the Democratic Progressive Alliance.
With affection,
M.K.
